norm_num

    intro h

    -- We have the hypothesis h: n + 2 ≤ n + 4.
    -- We can use omega or linarith to solve this.
    omega

norm_num
  | succ n ih =>
    -- Goal: n + 1 + 2 ≤ n + 1 + 4
    -- which simplifies to n + 3 ≤ n + 5
    -- This is true by omega
    omega

    omega

norm_num
  · -- Inductive step: Assume n + 2 ≤ n + 4, show (n + 1) + 2 ≤ (n + 1) + 4
    omega

norm_num
  · -- Inductive step: Assume k + 2 ≤ k + 4, prove (k + 1) + 2 ≤ (k + 1) + 4
    -- This simplifies to k + 3 ≤ k + 5, which is true if k + 2 ≤ k + 4
    -- The omega tactic can solve this kind of linear arithmetic goal
    omega

norm_num
  · -- Inductive step: assume k + 2 ≤ k + 4, prove (k + 1) + 2 ≤ (k + 1) + 4
    intro k ih
    omega

norm_num
  · -- Inductive step: assume n + 2 ≤ n + 4, prove (n + 1) + 2 ≤ (n + 1) + 4
    intro h
    simp [Nat.add_assoc, Nat.add_comm, Nat.add_left_comm] at h ⊢
    omega

have h2 : 2 ≤ 4 := by norm_num
    have h3 : n + 2 ≤ n + 4 := by
      apply Nat.add_le_add_left
      exact h2
    exact h3
  exact h1

norm_num
  | succ n ih =>
    simp at ih ⊢
    omega

norm_num
  · -- Inductive step
    rename_i n ih
    -- Goal: n + 2 ≤ n + 4
    -- Inductive hypothesis: n + 2 ≤ n + 4
    omega
